label: ai
topic: A lifestyle app that tells people what to do gains cultish popularity
text:
It started with Lauren refusing to order lunch without consulting her phone. We were sitting at our usual table by the window—the one that always had a faint draft and a sticky corner no one wanted to claim—and she held up her screen like it was a menu handed down from a higher authority.

"Abelique says I need more magnesium," she said. "So I'm getting the kale bowl."

"You hate kale," I said.

Lauren shrugged, already tapping to confirm the order. "I used to hate kale. That was before Abelique understood my body better than I did."

"Bleak," I said, but only half-joking.

Sam, across from us, nodded with unsettling seriousness. "It's not just food. It told me to stop watching crime shows after 9 p.m. because it spikes my cortisol. I slept through the night for the first time in years."

"That's because you weren't watching documentaries about serial killers before bed," I said.

Sam smiled, patient, as if I were a child resisting vegetables. "You could benefit from it, you know."

"I'm doing fine," I said, though even as I said it, I couldn't remember the last time I'd slept particularly well. Or eaten anything that wasn't beige.

The app had arrived quietly, like a new restaurant that suddenly everyone swears they discovered first. At first it was just ads—sleek, minimalist, promising "alignment" and "clarity." Then it was people at the office glancing at their phones before taking a bite of anything. Then it was our boss, Mark, forwarding an email titled "Wellness Initiative."

We got that email the following Monday.

"Team," it began, which is how you know something mildly invasive is about to happen, "we're excited to pilot Abelique as part of our workplace wellness program."

There was a meeting. There are always meetings. Mark stood at the front of the conference room, beaming like he'd personally invented circadian rhythms.

"This isn't mandatory," he said, which meant it absolutely was. "But Abelique can help optimize your productivity, mood, even collaboration."

"Does it tell us when to agree with each other?" someone asked.

Mark laughed a fraction too long. "In a way, yes. It helps align your communication patterns."

I looked around. Half the room was already nodding, phones in hand. The other half looked like me—mildly amused, vaguely uneasy, trying not to seem difficult.

By Thursday, people were scheduling meetings based on "cognitive peaks." By the following week, the break room had been quietly reorganized. The sugary snacks disappeared, replaced by labeled containers of almonds and something called "activated seeds." A sign appeared on the fridge: "Abelique-approved options only, please."

At lunch, our table began to fracture.

"I can't eat between 12:30 and 1:15 anymore," Sam said one day, checking his phone. "My digestion window closes."

"It closes?" I said. "What does that even mean?"

"It means my body processes nutrients more efficiently outside that window."

"Your body has been processing nutrients since you were born," I said.

Lauren didn't look up from her screen. "Abelique says you're being defensive."

I blinked. "Excuse me?"

"It analyzes conversational tone," she said. "It's actually really helpful."

"So now your app is critiquing me in real time?"

"It's not critiquing," Sam said gently. "It's offering insight."

I laughed, but it came out sharper than I intended. "Insight into what? Whether I should be allowed to finish a sentence?"

They exchanged a look—the kind people give when they've collectively decided you're missing something obvious.

That night, I downloaded Abelique.

Not because I believed in it. I told myself it was curiosity, anthropological interest. I wanted to see what all the fuss was about, to understand the logic behind it so I could dismantle it properly over lunch.

The interface was clean, almost aggressively so. It asked me questions in a tone that felt less like a survey and more like a gentle interrogation. Sleep habits. Food preferences. Emotional states, categorized with unnerving specificity.

When it finished, it paused, as if considering me.

Then: "You are out of alignment."

"Rude," I muttered.

It gave me a schedule. Wake at 6:42. Hydrate immediately. Avoid caffeine until 9:15. Engage in "low-resistance tasks" until 11:00. It suggested meals I would never choose, shows I had never heard of, and a bedtime so early it felt like an insult.

I ignored it.

The next morning, I woke at 6:39.

Not because of the app. I woke because I woke. But there it was, three minutes before its prescribed time, and for a moment—just a moment—I wondered if it meant something.

At work, Mark stopped by my desk.

"Hey," he said casually, which meant he had a purpose. "Have you tried Abelique yet?"

"I downloaded it," I said.

"And?"

"It thinks I'm out of alignment."

He smiled. "That's just the starting point. It gets more accurate the more you use it."

"Accurate about what?"

"About you."

I wanted to say that I already knew myself, that I didn't need an app to tell me when to eat lunch or how to feel. But the truth was murkier than that. There were gaps—small, persistent uncertainties about why I felt tired or restless or vaguely dissatisfied.

At lunch, I checked my phone before ordering.

"Don't," I said to myself, out loud.

Lauren looked up. "What?"

"Nothing," I said, and ordered the sandwich I always ordered.

It arrived, warm and familiar. I took a bite, and for a second, it tasted slightly wrong, as if I were eating something I'd already decided I shouldn't.

Across the table, Sam smiled at his salad. Lauren nodded at her bowl. Their phones rested beside their plates, quiet but present, like an extra guest at the table.

I chewed slowly, aware of my own chewing in a way I never had been before. I wondered what Abelique would say about this moment. Whether it would approve or gently correct me.

I didn't check.

But I thought about it.

And that, I realized, might be the point.
